coredata.dc:title: Strategic combination of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and g-C3N4: Efficient photocatalytic peroxydisulfate for the degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride and mechanism insight

coredata.dc:description:
Photo-assisted peroxydisulfate (PDS) activation has exhibited a great potential for pollution control. In this study, metal-free nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) modified g-C3N4 composite was fabricated and used to activate PDS under visible light to effectively remove tetracycline hydrochloride (HTC) in wastewater. Specifically, almost 90% of HTC (20 mg/L) was removed in 60 min under the conditions of an initial value of catalysis dose of 0.5 g/L and PDS dose of 0.6 g/L. Electron spin resonance analysis and trapping experiments confirmed that
SO4 −,
O2 − and h+ were found as the dominant contributors to the HTC degradation. PDS and N-CQDs acted as an electron acceptor to transfer the photogenerated charges, resulting in the successful activation of PDS. The promoting visible light absorption can be ascribed to up-conversion effect of N-CQDs. The combination strategy provides an innovative approach to synthesize binary metal-free carbonaceous photocatalysts for implementing PDS-based oxidative degradation of pollutants.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Photo-assisted peroxydisulfate (PDS) activation has exhibited a great potential for pollution control. In this study, metal-free nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) modified g-C3N4 composite was fabricated and used to activate PDS under visible light to effectively remove tetracycline hydrochloride (HTC) in wastewater. Specifically, almost 90% of HTC (20 mg/L) was removed in 60 min under the conditions of an initial value of catalysis dose of 0.5 g/L and PDS dose of 0.6 g/L. Electron spin resonance analysis and trapping experiments confirmed that
SO4 −,
O2 − and h+ were found as the dominant contributors to the HTC degradation. PDS and N-CQDs acted as an electron acceptor to transfer the photogenerated charges, resulting in the successful activation of PDS. The promoting visible light absorption can be ascribed to up-conversion effect of N-CQDs. The combination strategy provides an innovative approach to synthesize binary metal-free carbonaceous photocatalysts for implementing PDS-based oxidative degradation of pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para:
With the further development of industrialization, global environmental challenges have become one of the most critical problems. A series of antibiotics, particularly tetracyclines, have aroused great concerns due to their high toxicity, and non-degradability in the environment [1]. Recently, the advanced oxidation process based on sulfate radicals (•SO4 −) (SR-AOPs) has been considered as an encouraging strategy in the treatment of organic pollutants, due to the strong oxidation potential over a wide pH range and its relatively longer half-life [2]. These remarkable advantages endow
SO4 − with a strong oxidation ability to degrade contaminants effectively and completely. In this process, sulfate radicals (
SO4 −) or hydroxyl radicals (
OH) are generally generated by physically or chemically activation of peroxydisulfate (PDS) or peroxymonosulfate (PMS) [3,4]. To date, many methods have been devoted to activating PDS for
SO4 −, such as direct energy (heat, UV, ultrasound, etc.) or through the catalysis [5–8]. For example, both cathodic activation and anodic activation have been realized for electro-activation of PDS [9]. The piezoelectric effect is another approach to activate persulfate which shows great potential [10]. However among the reported methods, PDS activation triggered by photocatalysts has attracted great interest because it provides the potential to generate
SO4 − under mild reaction conditions and using green solar energy [11]. The critical for this heterogeneous SR-AOPs is seeking a photocatalyst with excellent activation performance and environmental friendliness.

As a metal-free polymer semiconductor nanomaterial, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) with visible-light responsiveness, high chemical stability, and unique π-conjugated structure has drawn tremendous attention for PDS or PMS activation [12]. In detail, g-C3N4 was excited under visible-light to produce photoinduced electrons, which can effectively activate PDS. Unfortunately, the poor electron transport, insufficient visible-NIR light utilization, and limited active sites restrict its application in activating PDS [13,14]. According to previous researches, pristine g-C3N4 presented poor activation capacity for PDS/PMS [15]. For example, Gao et al. indicated that the removal efficiency of the original g-C3N4 activated PMS system was far from reaching the expected target [16]. Liu et al. discovered that the enhanced catalytic activity was achieved by altering the g-C3N4 structure [17]. Therefore, it is urgent to explore the modification methods to enhance the PDS activation capability of g-C3N4-based heterogeneous SR-AOPs system [18–20].

There are also efforts aiming at improving the catalytic activity of these g-C3N4-based SR-advanced oxidation systems [21–25]. Among them, coupling with the transition metal-based materials is one of the widely used approach. Among them, cobalt, copper, iron, and manganese-based semiconductor materials have been applied in the activation of PDS/PMS [26,27]. For instance, Pi et al. discovered that the CoFeO2/g-C3N4 catalysts exhibited high oxidative degradation activity for levofloxacin through the activation of PMS [28]. Wang et al. studied that the g-C3N4/MnFe2O4-graphene composite activated PDS under visible light and exhibited superior catalytic degradation performance for the antibiotic wastewater [29]. Hu et al. proposed a g-C3N4/Fe (III)/PDS reaction to enhance the phenol degradation efficiency, which mainly caused by facilitating the generation of free radicals as well as accelerating the Fe (III)/Fe (II) cycle [30]. Furthermore, Fan et al. studied a Mn/g-C3N4 catalyst activated PMS to effectively degrade acetaminophen [21]. However, metal-based materials are generally expensive, and metal leaching can cause secondary pollution. Therefore, it is critical to find metal-free catalysts with excellent activation performance to couple with g-C3N4. Due to the excellent chemical stability, low-cost, and non-toxicity, carbon materials have drawn great attention [31]. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) retained the typical π-π* stacking transition of aromatic sp2 hybridized graphitic carbon, which exhibited various unique photophysical properties [32–34]. Besides, coupling CQDs with other photocatalysts can increase the light absorption through the up-conversion effect. Furthermore, it also can facilitate the transfer of photogenerated carriers. At the same time, because of the higher electronegativity of nitrogen compared with carbon, the charge distribution of carbon atoms adjacent to the substituted N dopant can be adjusted [16]. Thereby, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) display higher charge transfer ability than CQDs [32,35,36]. In view of the above advantages, the application of (N-)CQDs/g-C3N4 in the area of photocatalysis has been extensively studied [37–39]. Therefore, N-CQDs modified with g-C3N4 shows great prospects for PDS activation to degrade pollutants. However, the research regarding the N-CQDs/g-C3N4 as the efficient photocatalyst to activate PDS under visible light was rarely mentioned, and the insight mechanism of performance enhancement was still unclear. Therefore, exploring the application of N-CQDs/g-C3N4 materials to activate PDS under visible light is of great significance for wastewater treatment.

In this work, an N-CQDs/g-C3N4 catalyst was prepared through a simple hydrothermal strategy and applied for PDS activation under visible light for the removal of HTC wastewater. The structure, morphology and photoelectric properties of N-CQDs/g-C3N4 were investigated in detail. Subsequently, the effects of operation parameters on the catalytic degradation performance, including catalyst dosage, PDS concentration, pH, and coexisting anions, were explored. With the help of trapping experiments and electron spin resonance analysis, a reasonable enhancement mechanism was put forward. The reactive oxidative species (ROS) analyses further demonstrate that the photogenerated electrons were directly involved in the PDS activation process. This work can provide a novel perspective for green catalysis and develop a PDS-based AOPs system under visible light for efficient environmental modification.

Melamine, ammonium citrate, ethylenediamine, sodium peroxydisulfate (Na2S2O8, PDS), tetracycline hydrochloride (HTC), disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate dihydrate (EDTA-2Na), isopropanol (IPA), K2Cr2O7, 1,4-Benzoquinone (BQ), and methanol (MeOH) were purchased from Ainopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All chemicals were of analytical grade and no further purification was required.

The g-C3N4 was manufactured by heating melamine as the precursor in a muffle furnace according to the reported method [40]. Melamine was placed in a ceramic crucible under ambient pressure, and then it was heated to 550 °C for 4 h at a heating rate of 5 °C/min. The prepared yellow sample was the required g-C3N4.

N-CQDs were prepared from ammonium citrate through a hydrothermal strategy [33,37]. Typically, 10 mmol of ammonium citrate was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water containing 670 μL of ethylenediamine. Then, the mixture was placed into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 200 °C for 5 h and cooled naturally. The prepared solution was dialyzed using a semi-permeable membrane for 24 h (change the freshwater every eight hours) to prepare the N-CQDs solution.

The N-CQDs/g-C3N4 samples were obtained through a hydrothermal strategy. Specifically, an appropriate amount of N-CQDs solution was added to a beaker containing 40 mL of ethanol, where 0.25 g g-C3N4 was dispersed. After mixing well, the mixture was moved to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 393 K for 2 h. The N-CQDs/g-C3N4 samples were gathered through centrifugation and dried at 333 K overnight. Besides, a series of samples were obtained by adjusting the volume of the N-CQDs solution (1, 3, 4, and 5 mL) added to the mixture suspension. The prepared products were named N-CQDs/CN-1, N-CQDs/CN-3, N-CQDs/CN-4, N-CQDs/CN-5, respectively.

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded by a Bruker D8 Advance instrument diffractometer operating with Cu-Ka source. Surface electronic state was studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were studied with a NICO-LET 5700 FTIR spectrometer by using KBr pellets. The morphology of nanocomposites was characterized by transmission electron micrograph (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEOL JEM-2100F, Japan). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of nanocomposite was characterized by nitrogen absorption–desorption (ASAP2020, Micromerit, USA). The UV–vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy (DRS) was performed on a Shimadzu UV-3100 spectrophotometer using BaSO4 as the reference. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured with a Perkin-Elmer LS 55 using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The photoelectrochemical experiment was carried out on a CHI 660C electrochemical workstation by a standard three-electrode system. The FTO electrode with the sample deposited was used as the working electrode. Platinum wire and Ag/AgCl were regarded as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The electrolyte was 0.1 M Na2SO4. The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were conducted on a Bruker ER200-SRC spectrometer.

The catalytic performance of N-CQDs/g-C3N4 catalysts for activating PDS under visible light can be evaluated through the removal efficiency of HTC. Typically, 25 mg N-CQDs/g-C3N4 catalyst was added into a 50 mL HTC aqueous solution (20 mg/L). The visible light was simulated by a 300 W Xenon lamp with a 420 nm cutoff filter. The photocatalytic reactor was constantly located at 20 cm far away from the Xenon lamp. Before irradiation and addition of PDS, the above-mixed solution was subjected to dark reaction for 30 min to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Then, the prepared PDS was added and the resultant mixture was immediately exposed to visible light. During the photoreaction, 2.0 mL reaction solution was taken out every 10 min, rapidly mixed with 2.0 mL methanol to quench the reaction and filtered through the 0.22 μm filter. The residual HTC concentration was measured via a UV–vis spectrophotometer at the characteristic absorption peak of 357 nm.

The XRD analysis was commonly utilized to identify the different phase structure of samples (Fig. 1 a). Bulk g-C3N4 displayed two obvious diffraction peaks at 2θ = 27.6° and 13.0°, which was agreed with previous literature results [41]. The strong peak at 27.6° was assigned to the (0 0 2) diffraction with an interlayer spacing of 0.34 nm, representing the interlayer-stacking structure of the conjugated aromatic system [42]. The weaker peak at 13.0° originated from the in-plane structures of tri-s-triazine was indexed as (1 0 0) diffraction plane, corresponding to the interlayer spacing of 0.68 nm (JCPDS Card No. 87-1526) [43]. When increasing the amount of N-CQDs, the (0 0 2) diffraction peak moved toward the lower angle value (Fig. 1a inset), which corresponded to an augment in the lattice spacing of the layered structure [37,44,45]. This phenomenon indicated the successful coupling N-CQDs with g-C3N4. Furthermore, no obvious N-CQDs diffraction peak was observed in the XRD spectra of composite, which was mainly ascribed to the low crystallinity of N-CQDs or a small quantity dispersion on the g-C3N4 surface [35].

Fig. 1b displayed the typical FTIR spectra of the as-obtained products. There are no obvious differences among the FTIR spectra of g-C3N4 and N-CQDs/g-C3N4 catalysts. The C-N tensile vibration in the aromatic ring corresponded to vibration bands at 1000–1700 cm−1 [46]. The peak at 1404 cm−1 corresponded to the tensile and bending vibration of CH [47]. In addition, the characteristic absorption at 1625 cm−1 was involved in the CC tensile, which was attributed to the photoluminescence of N-CQDs caused by π-π* transition [44,48]. Whereas the characteristic absorption at 806 cm−1 mainly owed to the stretching vibration of the s-triazine unit [49]. Furthermore, the characteristic peaks related to N-CQDs were scarcely observed in the g-C3N4-based photocatalysts as previously reported in the literature [44]. This result indicated that the N-CQDs doping level was low, and the overall structure of g-C3N4 as the main body was not damaged in our work.

To better understand the interactions, the XPS technique was conducted to further verify the specific surface composition as well as elemental status. The survey spectrum of the N-CQDs/g-C3N4 catalyst (Fig. 1c) showed that only C, N), and O elements were observed. The changes of the surface functional groups were further discussed through high-resolution XPS spectra. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 1d) exhibited a wide peak of CC/CC at 285.2 eV [49]. The peak at 288.6 could be owed to NCN, the characteristic peak corresponding to CO (286.08 eV) displayed a slight shift (286.57 eV) after coupling with N-CQDs [46]. The COH on the surface of g-C3N4 and N-CQDs can bridge each other to form a COC bond through losing H2O molecules. The XPS results revealed that N-CQDs were anchored on the g-C3N4 by forming the CO bond. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1e) presented three obvious characteristic peaks at 399 eV, 400.5 eV, and 401.7 eV, corresponding to CNC, N(C)3, NH, respectively. While the remaining peak centered at 405.1 eV was caused by π excitation [39,50]. These N units constitute the N-CQDs/g-C3N4 basic structure, which was like the previously reported literature [38,51,52]. The O1s peaks (Fig. 1f) at 532.1 eV and 533.6 eV correspond to OC/OH and OC, which could be attributed to the absorbed water and oxygen components of N-CQDs, respectively [44,46]. Therefore, the above analysis indicated that N-CQDs was loaded on the g-C3N4 by the COC bond, and the primitive structure of g-C3N4 was unchanged.

The morphology and microstructure of catalysts were explored by TEM. As showed in Fig. 2 a–c, the pure g-C3N4 sample revealed its 2D lamellar structure and smooth surface, which was consistent with previous reports [53]. N-CQDs tended to be unevenly distributed, and evident dense black dot regions can be noticed on the g-C3N4 surface (Fig. 2d) [54]. The results further displayed the trend of agglomerate, and particles in close contact may induce lattice overlap (Fig. 2e). Furthermore, the HRTEM image of the crystal region showed that the interplanar spacing was 0.22 nm (Fig. 2f), which was consistent with the (1 0 1) lattice plane from graphitic carbon [49]. The observed lattice spacing was the same as that of the pure N-CQDs [33]. These results showed that N-CQDs was successfully coupled to the g-C3N4. The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of both g-C3N4 and N-CQDs/g-C3N4 composites displayed type IV curves, and a conspicuous hysteresis loop can be observed in the relative pressure region (P/P0) of 0.65–1.00 (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). It indicated the weak adsorption–desorption interaction and the mesoporous structure of the nanomaterials [37]. The specific surface area and pore volume of N-CQDs/CN-3 (12.205 m2/g and 0.055 cm3/g) were slightly smaller than bulk g-C3N4 (26.847 m2/g and 0.124 cm3/g). This might be caused by that N-CQDs occupied some spaces of bulk g-C3N4.

To investigate the altering of the light response region as well as bandgap energy of g-C3N4 after coupling with N-CQDs, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) and Kubella-Munk function vs. the photon energy of g-C3N4 and N-CQDs/CN-3 were displayed. As shown in Fig. 3 a, bulk g-C3N4 revealed an obvious absorption edge at about 475 nm, which was consistent with previous research [55]. However, N-CQDs/CN-3 demonstrated a slight red-shift and displayed stronger visible-light response. Moreover, the light absorption region of the N-CQDs/CN-3 almost extended to the near IR region. This can be ascribed to the expansion of electron delocalization in the aromatic sheets and strengthened interface structure connection [56]. Furthermore, the band gaps of the samples were directly narrowed after modified with N-CQDs and the Eg value of N-CQDs/CN-3 was reduced to 2.12 eV (Fig. 3b). The narrow bandgap can greatly enhance the utilization of the visible light, thereby promoting the generation of photoinduced carriers.

Photoluminescence (PL) analysis was conducted to study the photogenerated carriers transfer behavior (Fig. 3c). Generally, a lower PL intensity indicated excellent transfer efficiency of the photoinduced carries, resulting in superior catalytic performance. A remarkable fluorescence quenching was discovered for N-CQDs/CN-3 compared with bulk g-C3N4, revealing that N-CQDs modification greatly facilitated the transfer and separation of photoinduced carriers. The improved separation efficiency can be ascribed to the electron-withdrawing ability of N atoms in the conjugated C plane [34]. Obviously, the N-CQDs/CN-3 composite displayed a gradual blue shift in fluorescence emission. This was ascribed to the up-conversion effect of N-CQDs. The photoinduced carrier lifetime of bulk g-C3N4 and N-CQDs/CN-3 were further studied through the time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy in Fig. 3d. As shown in Table 1 , the average lifetime (τ), determined using the formula

, increases from 3.2550 ns in g-C3N4 to 3.5012 ns in N-CQDs/CN-3. Therefore, the prolonged lifetime of photoinduced carriers indicated that the introduction of N-CQDs could effectively reduce the carrier’s recombination and improve the photocarrier transport rate.

To further illustrate the photoinduced charge separation behaviors, the photoelectrochemical properties of the as-obtained catalysts were investigated. As observed in Fig. 4 a, the N-CQDs/CN-3 composite showed greater photocurrent intensity than g-C3N4, and N-CQDs/CN-3 displayed the highest value. The enhanced photocurrent intensity may be ascribed to the following reason, the N-CQDs was regarded as electron acceptors, and can efficiently capture the photoinduced electrons generated by the excitation of g-C3N4, thereby improving the separation and transfer efficiency of the photogenerated carriers. It was coincident with the PL measurement results.

In addition, the transfer efficiency of the photogenerated carriers was also studied in detail by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), as showed in Fig. 4b. The arc radius was closely related to the resistance of photogenerated charge transfer, the smaller radius indicated the lower charge transfer impedance and efficient transport of photogenerated carriers [57]. The arc radius of N-CQDs/CN-3 composite in the EIS Nyquist plots was much smaller than that of bulk g-C3N4, which implied the increase of electronic conductivity. Furthermore, N-CQDs/CN-3 exhibited the best electronic conductivity, which could be attributed to the achieved tight interface connection by adding an appropriate amount of N-CQDs. This analysis obviously showed that coupling of N-CQDs and g-C3N4 could efficiently accelerate interfacial electron transfer. Free electrons can effectively activate PDS, thereby greatly improving the catalytic performance of the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic HTC degradation in the N-CQDs/g-C3N4/PDS/vis system

ce:section.ce:para:
The PDS activation performances with g-C3N4 and different N-CQDs/g-C3N4 under visible light were studied by eliminating HTC as shown in Fig. 5
a and Fig S5. The distinct improvement for HTC degradation was achieved via the addition of PDS under visible light, which were much higher than N-CQDs/g-C3N4 without PDS. Moreover, only 73% HTC was eliminated in the g-C3N4/PDS/Vis reaction within 60 min. However, N-CQDs/CN-3 samples can degrade 90.0% HTC under the identical conditions. The removal efficiencies of all N-CQDs/g-C3N4 samples were improved, and their corresponding reaction rates were displayed in the following order: N-CQDs/CN-3 > N-CQDs/CN-4 > N-CQDs/CN-5 > N-CQDs/CN-1 > bulk g-C3N4. Excess N-CQDs were regarded as the recombination sites of electron-hole pairs. The above results were caused by that the N-CQDs modification can not only effectively extend visible-light response region through up-conversion effect, but also facilitate the rapid transfer of photoinduced carriers. Moreover, the free electrons generated by N-CQDs/g-C3N4 can activate PDS to generate
SO4 −, thereby further improving the degradation performance of the system. Considering the catalytic efficiency, N-CQDs/CN-3 was chosen to investigate the PDS activation for HTC degradation.

The comparative study of HTC degradation in the N-CQDs/g-C3N4/PDS AOPs system was conducted. As depicted in Fig. 5b, HTC removal caused by visible light irradiation or catalyst alone can be negligible. The comparative experiment indicated that the photodegradation efficiency of HTC was very low in the absence of PDS. In detail, only 25.03% HTC was eliminated in N-CQDs/CN-3 photocatalytic system. The unsatisfactory HTC degradation rate of N-CQDs/CN-3 without PDS was due to the limited surface area and active site. It is exciting that in the coexistence of PDS and N-CQDs/CN-3, the PDS activated by catalyst under visible light, which extremely enhanced the removal performance of HTC and 90.0% HTC was effectively removed. Obvious, the removal efficiencies of PDS/Vis and N-CQDs/CN-3/PDS were much lower than that of N-CQDs/CN-3/PDS/Vis, indicating the key role of N-CQDs/CN-3 catalyst and visible light irradiation. Therefore, we can draw a conclusion that a visible-light-driven activation of PDS by metal-free N-CQDs/g-C3N4 catalyst can greatly improve the HTC degradation efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS generation mechanism in the N-CQDs/g-C3N4/PDS/vis system
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the ROS generation mechanism in the N-CQDs/g-C3N4/PDS photocatalytic system, Mott-Schottky plots were investigated to ascertain the relative band alignment of g-C3N4 (Fig. S2, Supporting Information). The positive slopes of the tangent lines indicated that the g-C3N4 was typical n-type semiconductors [58]. Simultaneously, the flat band potential of pure g-C3N4 was calculated to be −1.1 eV (vs SCE). Thus, it was −0.9 versus the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE). Furthermore, the CB potential is about 0.1 lower than the flat band potential of the n-type semiconductor [59]. Hence, the CB potential of g-C3N4 was estimated to be −1.0 eV (vs NHE). Furthermore, the UV–vis DRS indicated the bandgap (Eg) value of 2.48 eV for g-C3N4 in this study. Therefore, the EVB value of g-C3N4 was + 1.48 eV (vs NHE).

Subsequently, radical trapping experiment was carried out to determine the ROS related to HTC removal of the N-CQDs/CN/PDS/Vis system (Fig. 8 a). When h+ was captured by EDTA-2Na, the removal efficiency of HTC was declined, indicating that h+ played a significant role in the oxidative degradation process. The CCl4 was added as the e- scavenger, and HTC removal efficiency do not perform obvious suppressing, indicating that photoinduced e- makes a little contribution to this reaction. Furthermore, in order to differentiate the different contributions of the
OH and
SO4 −. MeOH was added to capture the
OH and
SO4 − radicals in this system, and the HTC removal performance was significantly declined, while the HTC removal efficiency was slightly inhibited after adding
OH scavenger (IPA) [57]. It can be found that the inhibitory effect of MeOH on the removal efficiency was much greater than that of IPA. Therefore, the results suggested that the
SO4 − was responsible for the HTC degradation and
OH was not the critical radicals in the reaction. Meanwhile, the HTC degradation rate was greatly decreased with the addition of p-BQ, implying that the
O2 − also played a leading role in the oxidative degradation reaction.

Electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis was generally conducted to examine the production of
O2 −,
OH, and
SO4 − in the reaction systems [33]. The DMPO-
O2 − over g-C3N4 and N-CQDs/g-C3N4 were observed under visible light illumination (Fig. 8b). The characteristic peaks intensity of DMPO-
O2 − on N-CQDs/g-C3N4 was much stronger than that of g-C3N4, indicating that N-CQDs coupling can extremely improve the molecular oxygen activation capacity of N-CQDs/g-C3N4 composites. Likewise, the four characteristic peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 could also be noticed, corresponding to the DMPO-
OH (Fig. 8c). It showed that the peak intensity of DMPO-
OH was significantly enhanced after modifying the N-CQDs on g-C3N4, implying more hydroxyl radical production. The ESR spectra of N-CQDs/g-C3N4/PDS/Vis and g-C3N4/PDS photocatalytic system displayed four- and six-line signals, corresponding to the characteristic peaks of DMPO-
OH and DMPO-
SO4 −, respectively (Fig. 8d). The results revealed that the N-CQDs doping can extremely facilitate the activation of PDS under visible light due to the enhanced separation of photogenerated charges and promoted light absorption, which was beneficial to yield
SO4 − radicals. Furthermore, the intensity of the
OH signal for the N-CQDs/g-C3N4/PDS/Vis was slightly stronger than that of g-C3N4/Vis and N-CQDs/g-C3N4 photocatalytic system, suggesting that more hydroxyl species were produced, which can be attributed to the conversion of •SO4 − radicals. The above mentioned showed that PDS can be effectively activated to promote the generation of sulfate and hydroxyl species in N-CQDs/g-C3N4/PDS photocatalytic system, thereby further improving the degradation efficiency. Because the photodegradation reaction mainly counted on these active radicals, higher concentrations of active radicals can lead to higher removal efficiency. This study showed that
O2 −,
SO4 − and
OH radicals were produced in the N-CQDs/g-C3N4/PDS/Vis system. This result was consistent with the trapping experiment.

According to the above discussions, the enhancement mechanism of the HTC removal efficiency in the N-CQDs/g-C3N4/PDS photocatalytic system was illustrated in Fig. 9 . After N-CQDs modification, the light absorption ability of N-CQDs/g-C3N4 was effectively enhanced through up-conversion effect. Under visible light irradiation, the electrons were excited from the VB of g-C3N4 to the CB, and the corresponding holes were retained on the VB (Eq. (1)). Simultaneously, due to the strong electron affinity of N atoms in N-CQDs [32], the N-CQDs could serve as electron traps to capture electrons from the CB of g-C3N4 through the junction interface, which thus enhancing the separation of electron-hole charges (Eq. (2)) [30]. Subsequently, the accumulated electrons on the N-CQDs and the CB of g-C3N4 could react with electrophilic oxygen of PDS, which can break the OO bond and produce sulfate radicals (Eqs. (3), (4)) [56]. Moreover, the accumulated electrons can also reduce the free O2 to produce
O2 − (Eq. (5, 6)) because the energy levels of the N-CQDs and g-C3N4 were more negative than the reduction potential of oxygen (E0
O2 − / O2 = -0.046 eV, vs. NHE). Then, the
O2 − radicals could combine with S2O8 2− molecules to bring out
SO4 − (Eq. (7)). In addition, the VB of g-C3N4 (+1.48 eV) was more negative than the standard redox potential of
OH/OH− (+2.70 eV) and
OH/H2O (+2.73 eV) [60]. Therefore, the residual h+ can't directly oxidize OH− or H2O to produce
OH, but can degrade pollutants absorbed on the catalyst surface. However, ESR and capture experiments of active radicals confirmed the presence of
OH radicals in the reaction, which may be caused by further reduction of
O2 −. The
O2 − species can combine with H2O molecules to produce
OH (Eq. (8)). More
OH species were produced by the interaction between
SO4 − and OH− (H2O) (Eq. (9)). In summary,
SO4 −,
O2 −, h+ and
OH radicals all participate in this N-CQDs/g-C3N4/PDS/Vis system, resulting in efficient degradation of HTC (Eq. (10)).
(1) g-C3N4 + hv → g-C3N4 (e−) + g-C3N4 (h+)
(2) g-C3N4 (e−) + N-CQDs → g-C3N4 + N-CQDs (e−)
(3) g-C3N4 (e−) + S2O8 2−→
SO4 −+SO4 2−
(4) N-CQDs (e−) + S2O8 2−→ N-CQDs +•SO4 − + SO4 2−
(5) g-C3N4 (e−) + O2 → g-C3N4 +
O2 −
(6) N-CQDs (e−) + O2 → N-CQDs + •O2 −
(7) S2O8 2− +
O2 − →
SO4 − + SO4 2− + O2
(8) 2H2O +
O2 − → 2OH− +2
OH
(9)
SO4 - + OH− (H2O) → SO4 2− +
OH (+H+)
(10)
SO4 −,
O2 −, h+,
OH + HTC → products

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of catalyst dosage, PDS dosage, pH and coexisting anions on HTC degradation
ce:section.ce:para: From an economic cost perspective, catalyst usage is a critical factor in pollutant treatment. Fig. 6a displayed that the removal rate increased gradually as the amount of the catalyst increase from 0 to 0.5 g/L. However, the HTC degradation efficiency exhibited a slight decline with further increase the catalyst usage, which confirmed that 0.5 g/L was the optimal catalyst usage in the catalytic system. The excessive catalyst may result in higher turbidity in the degradation system, thereby shielding light absorption and reducing light utilization. Therefore, less visible light can be utilized to induce photogenerated charge carries for the further free radical generation and degradation of HTC.

Fig. 6b showed the influence of PDS concentration on HTC degradation. The degradation performance of HTC was extremely improved with the increase of PDS concentration from 0.2 to 0.6 g/L. During the PDS activation process, photogenerated charges can be transfer to generate
SO4 − hence improving the degradation performance. However, a further increasing of PDS concentration may lead to a slight decreased removal activity. It could be probably ascribed to that excess PDS can react with
SO4 − to form
S2O8 − through Eq. (11). The generated
S2O8 − radicals were less active than
SO4 - and even exhibited a negative influence on the production of
SO4 − via Eq. (12) [5]. Furthermore, the catalyst usage may also be a limiting factor at higher PDS concentrations. Limited catalyst can only activate part of the PDS in the reaction system [60]. Consequently, 0.6 g/L was selected as the optimum concentration of PDS to realize the cost-effectiveness.
(11) S2O8 2− +
SO4 − → SO4 2− +
S2O8 −
(12)
SO4 − +
SO4 − → S2O8 2−
As illustrated in Fig. 6c, the degradation rate decreased slightly under strong acidic and strong alkaline conditions. At pH = 2.0, 77.35% HTC was eliminated in 60 min. At pH = 4.0, 80.79% degradation rate was achieved. 90.0% and 88.20% HTC were eliminated at the initial pH (6.26) and 6.0, respectively. It is obviously that the acidic condition is not favorable for HTC removal under the vis/PDS system. Previous results have pointed out that the surface charge presented a hindrance to the overall catalytic activity through limit the successful contact between HTC molecular with PDS [61]. Constantly, the abundant hydroxyl groups at the surface of the materials can also facilitate redox catalysis. At lower pH, the surface hydroxyl groups are disrupted due to the formation of protonated surface hydroxyl groups [62]. In addition, the photo-generated ∙O2– was more likely to combine with the abundant H+ in the solution rather than the HTC molecules, thus seriously hindering the degradation efficiency. While the HTC degradation efficiency was declined significantly upon the solution pH increased to 10.0, and only 70% HTC was removed within 60 min. This result may cause by the strong adsorption of the HTC on the surface of the photocatalyst. As reported in previous studies, an excessive adsorption can block light from arriving at the catalyst surface, reducing the surface light intensity, and impeding the hole-electron photoexcitation process subsequently. Moreover, the
SO4 − and be reacted with OH− to generate
OH with weaker redox capacity at high pH environmental, thereby decreasing the removal efficiency under alkaline conditions [63]. In this study, it showed that HTC exhibited the maximum degradation rate at an unadjusted initial pH (6.26). Therefore, partial neutral conditions were conducive to the efficient degradation of HTC in the N-CQDs/CN/PDS/Vis system. However, the catalytic degradation reaction can be carried out efficiently in a wide pH range of 2.0–10.0, which indicated that it can be widely used in the treatment of organic wastewater.

Various inorganic anions have always existed in practical wastewater, such as Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 − and CO3 2−. Therefore, it is essential to study the effect of coexisting anions in this system on HTC removal efficiency. 10 mM different metal salts (NaCl, Na2SO4, NaNO3 and NaCO3) were added separately to the CTC-HCl solution to provide the inorganic anions for Cl-, SO4 2-, NO3 – and CO3 2–. As showed in Fig. 6d, both Cl- and CO3 2– exhibit significant suppression effects with different degrees. It has been reported that Cl− can be oxidized to less reactive chlorine or hypochlorous by
SO4 − or
OH during the photocatalytic process. Thereby, the existence of NaCl had a great negative impact on the HTC elimination (Eq. (13)) [5]. Obviously, CO3 2− exhibited an inhibitory effect on the HTC elimination, its removal rate was reduced to 75.22% in the existence of CO3 2−. The •OH with lower oxidation potential can be quickly produced through the reaction of
SO4 − and OH− (Eq. (14)). The CO3 2− in this system could capture the
SO4 − radicals (Eq. (15)). In other words, the competitive reaction between CO3 2− and PDS may be the chief reason for the decreased catalytic performance in this system [5]. Different from other anions, SO4 2− showed little effect on the degradation of HTC due to its difficulty to react with
SO4 − [63]. The NO3 − also brought out a slight inhabitation since it can also be used as a trapping agent for
SO4 − or
OH species and form nitrate species by electron transfer (Eq. (16) and (17)).
(13)
SO4 − + Cl−→ SO4 2− +
Cl
(14)
SO4 − + OH− →
OH + SO4 2−
(15) CO3 2− +
SO4 −→ SO4 2− +
CO3 −
(16)
SO4 − + NO3 − →
NO3 + SO4 2−
(17) NO3 − +
OH →
NO3 + OH−

The chemical stability as well as the cycle performance of the catalysts are significant factors for its practical application. Repeated experiments for HTC removal were carried out to investigate the photostability of N-CQDs/g-C3N4 in the PDS reaction system. The removal efficiency of N-CQDs/g-C3N4 decreased slightly after five successive cycles (Fig. 7a), the π-π stacking interactions between N-CQDs and g-C3N4 result in its excellent photochemical stability [64]. Moreover, the XRD pattern of the used catalyst was not significantly different from that of the fresh, indicating the structure of catalysts was hardly changed in the reaction (Fig. 7b). The results indicated that N-CQDs/g-C3N4 catalysts had good reusability and can be used in practical pollutant treatment.

In summary, the N-CQDs coupled g-C3N4 catalyst exhibited superior PDS activation performance under visible light, and can efficiently oxidize and degrade HTC in wastewater. Compared with g-C3N4/Vis, N-CQDs/g-C3N4/Vis and g-C3N4/PDS reaction systems, N-CQDs/g-C3N4/PDS photocatalytic system displayed an optimal HTC removal rate (90.0%) within 60 min. Parameters in the reaction of the N-CQDs/g-C3N4/PDS photocatalytic system were deeply considered, including catalyst dosage, PDS dosage, pH, and coexisting ions. The generation of •SO4 −, •O2 − and h+ radicals was essential for the improvement of catalytic oxidation performance in the system. Furthermore, the N-CQDs/g-C3N4 catalyst displayed excellent chemical stability and recyclability. The synergistic effect between N-CQDs, g-C3N4 and PDS was summarized as three aspects. The promoting visible light absorption can be ascribed to up-conversion effect of N-CQDs. Then, the enhanced separation rate of photogenerated charges carries was caused by N-CQDs as the electron traps. Finally, PDS can also act as the electron acceptor to boost more •SO4 − and •O2 − radicals. In conclusion, the activation of PDS by metal-free N-CQDs/g-C3N4 under visible light provides a practical mean for the efficient degradation of HTC.
